options.why: Compared the raw digit strings and treated $17$ as bigger than $2$, ignoring place value.
Assumed writing $0.2$ as $0.20$ changes its value instead of recognising trailing zeros don't.
Believed decimals need the same number of places before they can be compared at all.
Compared only the first decimal digit and treated $0.6$ and $0.605$ as the same size.
Ordered smallest to largest instead of the largest-to-smallest order asked for.
Compared digit strings by length, ranking the longest decimal in the middle.
Treated a shift in place value (tenths vs hundredths) as if it didn't change the value.
Assumed moving the decimal point doesn't change the value of the number.
Confused tenths with thousandths, missing two place-value columns.

questions.q: A number between $0.42$ and $0.43$ has three decimal places. Which of these **could** it be?

options.why: Gave a value greater than $0.43$, outside the upper bound of the interval.
Gave a value less than $0.42$, outside the lower bound of the interval.
Repeated the lower bound itself rather than a value strictly between the two given decimals.
